questions.q: In the Geiger-Marsden gold-foil experiment, most alpha particles passed straight through the foil. What does this observation establish about the atom?
options: The atom has a diffuse, uniform positive charge
The atom is mostly empty space
Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed energy levels
The alpha particles are attracted to the nucleus
questions.explanation: Most alphas passing straight through shows the atom is mostly empty space. The rare large-angle deflections and rebounds showed the positive charge is concentrated in a tiny, dense, massive nucleus. (Like charges, so the alpha is repelled, not attracted.)

questions.q: Why did the Rutherford (planetary) model of the atom fail, according to classical electromagnetism?
options: It could not account for the mass of the nucleus
An orbiting electron is accelerating, so it must radiate energy, spiral inward and collapse — predicting unstable atoms and a continuous spectrum, contrary to observation
It predicted the atom had no empty space
It required electrons to have a wavelength
questions.explanation: An orbiting electron is centripetally accelerating, and an accelerating charge radiates electromagnetic waves. The model therefore predicts the electron spirals into the nucleus, giving unstable atoms and a continuous spectrum — but atoms are stable and emit sharp discrete lines. Bohr's postulates were introduced to fix this.

questions.q: Electrons fired at a crystal produce a diffraction pattern. What conclusion does this force, and what deeper question does it answer?
options: Electrons are particles; it explains their charge
Electrons behave as waves (de Broglie matter waves); only whole-number-wavelength orbits form standing waves, explaining why Bohr's orbits are quantised
Electrons have no mass; it explains beta decay
Electrons are made of quarks; it explains the Standard Model
questions.explanation: Diffraction is a wave behaviour, confirming de Broglie's matter waves. The payoff: only orbits whose circumference is a whole number of electron wavelengths form a stable standing wave, which is the mechanism Bohr asserted but could not justify for quantised orbits. Just quoting the wavelength formula earns little — the standing-wave argument is the mark.

questions.q: What are the quark compositions of a proton and a neutron?
options: Proton = up-down-down; neutron = up-up-down
Proton = up-up-down; neutron = up-down-down
Proton = up-up-up; neutron = down-down-down
Proton = up-down; neutron = up-up
questions.explanation: Proton = uud: \(+\tfrac{2}{3} + \tfrac{2}{3} - \tfrac{1}{3} = +1\). Neutron = udd: \(+\tfrac{2}{3} - \tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{1}{3} = 0\). Doing the charge sum confirms the assignment and earns an easy mark.

questions.q: Compare the ionising power and penetrating ability of alpha, beta and gamma radiation.
options: Alpha is the most penetrating and least ionising; gamma is the least penetrating
All three have equal ionising power but different penetration
Alpha is the most ionising but least penetrating; gamma is the least ionising but most penetrating (the two are inversely related)
Beta is the most ionising and most penetrating
questions.explanation: Alpha (helium nucleus) is most ionising but stopped by paper; gamma (a photon) is weakly ionising but needs centimetres of lead; beta is intermediate. Ionising power and penetration are inversely related — the most ionising radiation deposits its energy fastest and so stops soonest.

questions.q: At the quark level, what happens in beta-minus decay, and which force is responsible?
options: An up quark becomes a down quark, via the strong force
A down quark becomes an up quark, via the weak force (W boson), turning a neutron into a proton and emitting an electron and antineutrino
A proton is split into three quarks, via electromagnetism
A gluon is emitted, via gravity
questions.explanation: In beta-minus decay a neutron becomes a proton: a down quark changes to an up quark, mediated by the weak nuclear force (W boson), emitting an electron and an antineutrino. Mass number is unchanged, atomic number rises by one. Beta decay is the weak force, not the strong force.

questions.q: Why does the binding-energy-per-nucleon curve explain why BOTH fission and fusion release energy, and why fusion stops at iron?
options: Both reactions move products away from the iron-56 peak, releasing energy; iron is too light to fuse
Both reactions move products toward the iron-56 peak (higher binding energy per nucleon, lighter products, energy released); iron is at the peak, so fusing past it would require energy input
Fusion releases energy but fission absorbs it; iron cannot exist in stars
Both reactions destroy mass entirely; iron has no binding energy
questions.explanation: Fusion (light nuclei climbing the left) and fission (heavy nuclei climbing the right) both produce nuclei higher on the curve, nearer iron-56, so the products are more tightly bound and lighter — the mass defect is released via \(E = mc^2\). Iron-56 is the peak, so fusing it or heavier nuclei moves down the curve and absorbs energy, halting fusion.

questions.q: A galaxy's hydrogen absorption lines are shifted toward longer wavelengths. What does this single spectrum reveal, and what extra fact is needed to conclude the universe is expanding?
options: The galaxy is approaching; no extra fact is needed
The galaxy contains hydrogen and is receding (redshift); to conclude expansion you also need that redshift increases with distance (recession speed proportional to distance)
The galaxy is made of iron; the universe is contracting
The galaxy is hot; the universe is static
questions.explanation: The recognisable hydrogen line pattern shows composition; its shift to longer wavelengths (redshift) shows the galaxy is receding. But a single redshift is just motion — only the fact that redshift INCREASES WITH DISTANCE (Hubble's law) implies space itself is stretching, i.e. the expanding universe.

questions.q: Why must positron-electron annihilation produce two gamma photons in opposite directions?
options: To conserve charge, since each photon is charged
To conserve momentum: the pair is nearly at rest (total momentum near zero), and a single photon always carries momentum, so two opposite photons are needed to cancel to zero
Because two photons carry more energy than one
Because the strong force requires paired emission
questions.explanation: The annihilating pair is essentially at rest, so total momentum is near zero. A single photon always carries momentum and could not give zero total, violating conservation of momentum. Two equal photons emitted back-to-back have momenta that cancel. Each carries 0.511 MeV — the cleanest demonstration of \(E = mc^2\).

questions.q: Evaluate the claim 'all the chemical elements were made in stars.' Which response best reflects the three-stage origin of the elements?
options: Fully true — every element was forged by stellar fusion
Fully false — all elements came from the Big Bang
Partly true but imprecise: the Big Bang made hydrogen, helium and trace lithium; stars made elements up to iron; supernovae made elements heavier than iron
Partly true: stars made the light elements and supernovae made everything up to iron
questions.explanation: The claim is partly true but imprecise. Hydrogen, helium and a trace of lithium formed in the Big Bang; stellar fusion built elements up to iron-56 (the binding-energy peak); and elements heavier than iron formed in supernovae by rapid neutron capture. 'Iron is the heaviest element made by fusion' is the key qualifier.
